coredata.dc:title: Oxygen vacancy and double carbon modification of WO3 for enhancing the photoelectric performance
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Linhui
Lei, Yun
Li, Can
Du, Beibei
Wang, Yongqin
Deng, Yifan
Tang, Zehui
Chen, Jiong
Lin, Li
coredata.dc:description: WO3 and N-GQDs were loaded in a two-dimensional graphene sheet layer by hydrothermal method, and then oxygen vacancy-rich WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites were successfully prepared by calcination. The structures, morphologies and surface chemical states were characterized by XRD, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and the electrochemical properties were studied by EIS, i-t, LSV and M-S. The WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites had a high carrier density of 5.88 × 1020 cm−3 and presented an instantaneous photocurrent density of 2.96 × 10−4 A/cm2 under 365 nm irradiation, which was 4.4 times larger than that of pure WO3. The enhanced properties were mainly ascribed to the increased light absorption and carrier migration by the two-carbon modification of N-GQDs and N-rGO. Meanwhile, the presence of oxygen vacancies can improve the carrier lifetime, thus suppressing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites. The photoelectric properties of the composites were improved by the cooperative effect of double carbon modification and oxygen vacancies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: WO3
oxygen vacancy
Double carbon modification
Photoelectric properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Key Laboratory for Research and Development of new lithium-ion battery materials (2023-028) and open foundation of Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei University .

The work was supported by Key Laboratory for Research and Development of new lithium-ion battery materials (2023-028) and Joint Fund of Bijie City and Guizhou University of Engineering Science (BiKeLianhe[2023]12th).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites.
UV–Vis DRS (a) and Tauc plots (b) of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
PL spectra (a) and Time-resolved transient PL decay curve (b) of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
Fourier infrared spectra of N-GQDs, WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
Transmission electron microscopy images of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (a–d).
XPS full spectra (a) and high-resolution maps of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), O 1s (d) and W 4f (e) of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
Photocurrent response curves of WO3/N-GQDS composites with different N-GQDs composite proportions (a) and the photocurrent response curves of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (b).
Electrochemical impedance diagrams of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
Linear sweep voltammetry curve of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO under light irradiation at 365 nm.
Mott-Schottky diagram of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.
Charge transfer schematic of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites.

head.ce:title: Oxygen vacancy and double carbon modification of WO3 for enhancing the photoelectric performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou University of Engineering Science, Bijie City, 551700, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou University of Engineering Science, Bijie City, 551700, Peoples R China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, Peoples R China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Guizhou University of Engineering Science, Bijie, 551700, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: WO3 and N-GQDs were loaded in a two-dimensional graphene sheet layer by hydrothermal method, and then oxygen vacancy-rich WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites were successfully prepared by calcination. The structures, morphologies and surface chemical states were characterized by XRD, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and the electrochemical properties were studied by EIS, i-t, LSV and M-S. The WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites had a high carrier density of 5.88 × 1020 cm−3 and presented an instantaneous photocurrent density of 2.96 × 10−4 A/cm2 under 365 nm irradiation, which was 4.4 times larger than that of pure WO3. The enhanced properties were mainly ascribed to the increased light absorption and carrier migration by the two-carbon modification of N-GQDs and N-rGO. Meanwhile, the presence of oxygen vacancies can improve the carrier lifetime, thus suppressing the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in the WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites. The photoelectric properties of the composites were improved by the cooperative effect of double carbon modification and oxygen vacancies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The n-type semiconductor tungsten oxide (WO3) is considered an ideal photoelectric anode material due to its narrow and tunable band gap, visible light response, high oxidation resistance, and structural stability [1]. WO3 nanomaterials are widely used in colloidal quantum dot luminescence technology [2], solar cells [3], and photocatalysis [4,5]. However, the high recombination rate of pure WO3 photogenerated carriers largely limits their application in the field of photoelectrochemistry. Currently, there are many methods to inhibit WO3 electron-hole complexation and promote carrier separation efficiency, such as doping elements in WO3 crystals, designing defect vacancies, and constructing junction structures. Among them, the introduction of oxygen vacancies to enhance the photoelectric properties of WO3 is considered to be a simple and effective strategy by enhancing the electrical conductivity, visible light absorption and localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of WO3 [6–8]. Also, the introduction of oxygen vacancies facilitates the separation of photogenerated electrons from holes, which contributes to the improvement of photochemical performance. Wang et al. [9] reported that appropriate oxygen vacancy on the WO3 surface could improve its photocatalytic hydrolysis efficiency. Zhang et al. [10] found that WO3 nanofibers also showed a good response to NO2 at lower temperatures after constructing oxygen vacancies on them. Yang et al. [11] employed hydrogen treatment to obtain WO3 with oxygen vacancies, which exhibited good photocurrent response performance.

As a new 2D material, graphene has received much attention for its excellent physical, chemical and optical properties, while the zero-band gap and easy agglomeration of intrinsic graphene limit its application. The electronic structure of intrinsic graphene can be changed by opening the band gap and changing the surface chemistry of graphene through doping [12]. Graphene-based semiconductors can effectively improve the material properties since the attachment of semiconductors to the graphene surface reduces the agglomeration of graphene sheets and semiconductor nanoparticles. In addition, graphene-based sheets with the excellent electrical conductivity can act as electron migration channels and inhibit the electron-hole recombination of semiconductors. Ismail et al. [13] found that the synergistic effect between WO3 and GO sheets effectively suppressed the complexation of electron-hole pairs in Pt/WO3-GO nanocomposites. Tie et al. [14] prepared Pt/GO-ZnS composites with a high photocatalytic activity using the synergistic effect of GO and Pt on ZnS.

Graphene quantum dots have obvious quantum confinement and edge effects due to their quasi-zero dimensional size at the nanoscale and have outstanding application prospects in bioimaging [15], electrochemical detection [16] and photocatalysis [17]. The light absorption range and fluorescence efficiency of graphene quantum dots can be further enhanced by doping with N, S, P, B, and other elements [18,19]. Among them, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high quantum yield and surface active sites are considered to promote the visible light absorption in modified semiconductor materials. For example, Zhang et al. [20] found that the light absorption range of N-GQDs/BiOX composites modified by N-GQDs was red-shifted and the visible light absorption intensity was enhanced. Jiang et al. [21] used N-GQDs as a photosensitizer to enhance the light absorption intensity of ZCS/NG/GR composites and improve the photocurrent response of the material.

Although the performance of semiconductors can be improved to some extent by oxygen vacancies or by forming heterojunctions with carbon materials, a single modification pathway is limited if new strategies are required to be further exploited. Based on this, N-rGO and N-GQDs are used to improve the optoelectronic properties of WO3 materials by double carbon modification and oxygen vacancy adjustment in the materials. Among them, N-GQDs act as a photosensitizer to improve the light absorption efficiency of the composites, N-rGO acts as an electron migrating carrier to effectively promote the charge migration, and the presence of oxygen vacancies can trap photoelectrons and extend the carrier lifetime. Therefore, in this paper, WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites were prepared by a simple hydrothermal and calcination process, and investigated by a series of characterizations. The effects of oxygen vacancies and graphene-based carbon materials on the enhancement of the photoelectric properties of the composites were studied and the charge transfer mechanism was analyzed.
The reagents used in the experiments are analytically pure and can be used directly.

First, 60 mg of graphite oxide (synthesized by the Hummers' method) was dispersed in 120 ml of deionized water by ultrasonic treatment, and then 2.4 ml ammonia and 8 ml hydrogen peroxide were added. After 30 min of sonication, the solution was poured into a 200 mL reactor and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. Large particles were removed with a 200-μm filter membrane, and then the filtrate was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (3000Da) for 48 h to obtain N-GQDs (1 mg/mL).

3 g urea and 300 mg graphite oxide were dispersed in 120 mL of deionized water, and the solution was sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was poured into an autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the graphite oxide was collected by filtration and washed with DI water, and finally dried at 60 °C.

570 mg oxalic acid and 1 g sodium tungstate were added to 60 ml of deionized water, and an appropriate amount of N-GQDs and N-rGO was added after sonication for 30 min. As the pH was adjusted to 1, the solution was sonicated for 30 min to make the solution homogeneous. The obtained solution was transferred to a reaction maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. The solid was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. The obtained product was placed in a tube furnace and calcined at 300 °C under nitrogen protection for 1 h to obtain WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO. WO3 and WO3/N-GQDs were prepared by the same method except that N-GQDs and N-rGO were not added in the preparation process.

The structure and morphology of the materials were examined using TEM (FEI Tecnai F20) and XRD (D8 Advance). To obtain the functional group and elemental information, FTIR (Nicolet, IS-10) and XPS (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) analysis was used. UV–vis (Lambda 750 S) was used to study the optical properties of materials. The instantaneous photocurrent responses, Nyquist and Mott-Schottky curves of the samples were carried out on a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI650E) with the Pt electrode, Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode and sample-coated FTO glass as the counter electrode, the reference electrode and the working electrode, respectively.

XRD experiments were done to investigate the physical phase of the samples, and the findings were given in Fig. 1 . Three strong WO3 peaks emerged at 23.12°, 23.61°, and 24.37°, corresponding to the crystal planes of WO3 (JCPDS No. 43–1035), and other diffraction peaks can be matched with the standard card. The peaks were strong and crisp, with no impurities, indicating that the crystallinity of WO3 was good. According to the Bragg equation, the crystal plane spacings corresponding to the three strong peaks were 0.38 nm, 0.37 nm, and 0.36 nm, respectively. When N-GQDs were compounded, additional diffraction peaks appear at 18.09°, 27.08°, 28.11°, and 36.72°, which were ascribed to orthorhombic WO3.0.33H2O (JCPDS No. 35–0270). It may be attributed to the N-GQDs doping which produced oxygen vacancies and decreased crystal parameters and induced a phase transition [22–24]. In contrast, the peak intensity of orthorhombic phase WO3 of the double carbon modified WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites was reduced, which may be attributed to the reason that some of the N-GQDs were attached to the N-rGO surface, and affected the crystallization of WO3 during the compounding process [25]. The diffraction peaks associated with broad graphene were not found in the composites. It may be attributed to the low intensity of the diffraction peaks caused by the low content of N-GQDs and N-rGO.

The UV–vis DRS was performed on WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption edges of all the samples were in the visible range, and the absorption intensity of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO could be improved by the introduction of N-GQDs and N-rGO, in which N-GQDs served as sensitizers for enhancing the visible-light absorption ability [26]. In addition, the optical band gap (Eg) of the material was calculated based on the Kubelkae-Munk method and the results were shown in Fig. 2b. The band gap of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO were 2.66, 2.63, and 2.61 eV, respectively. The addition of N-GQDs and N-rGO led to a decrease in the band gap of the composites and an increase in light absorption.

PL spectra and time-resolved transient PL (TRPL) decay curves of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs, and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO were performed and the results were shown in Fig. 3
. A peak at about 486 nm was ascribed to the fluorescence emission of WO3 [27]. The fluorescence intensity of WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was lower than that of WO3, which suggested that N-GQDs and N-rGO effectively reduced the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. TRPL decay spectra were shown in Fig. 3b. WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO and WO3/N-GQDs exhibited faster PL decay compared to WO3, which indicated more efficient separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers in the composites [28]. The presence of N-GQDs and N-rGO in the composites contributed to the quick transfer that promoted the nonradiative decay process, leading to a certain inhibition of the photogenerated electron and hole recombination in the radiative relaxation process.

In the FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 4), the relatively obvious –OH related vibrational peak near 3400 cm−1 was due to the adsorbed water on the sample surface. The 1640 cm−1 peak corresponding to the CO bond was observed, indicating that oxygen-containing functional groups were present in the N-GQDs prepared by hydrothermal method. The observed vibrational peaks at 1400 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 corresponded to the relevant vibrations of C–N and CC bonds [29], which suggested the successful doping of N elements into the GQDs. The vibrational peaks of WO3 were observed at 750 cm−1 and 828 cm−1, which were assigned to the WO and W–O–W stretching vibrations [30], respectively. For WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO, the stretching vibrational peak of N–H bond was observed at 3240 cm−1, which was derived from N-rGO [31], demonstrating the presence of N-rGO in the WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites.

Fig. 5 a indicated that WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was made up of nanosheets. Fig. 5b–d showed HRTEM images of the composites. The 0.38 nm and 0.37 nm crystal plane spacings of WO3 corresponded to the (002) and (020) crystal planes which were in general agreement with the theoretical values calculated by the Bragg equation, whereas the 0.34 nm, 0.24 nm and 0.19 nm crystal plane spacings of N-GQDs corresponded to the (002), (1120) and (104) crystal planes. In addition, the lattice planes of the composites become fuzzy (red circle region) in Fig. 5d, which might be owing to surface defects generated by oxygen vacancies during the annealing process of WO3 with N-GQDs and N-rGO [32,33].

The same elements, C, O, and W were detected in the full XPS photoelectron spectra of WO3, WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (Fig. 6 a), while the element N was only detected in WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO. The spectrum of C1s of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (Fig. 6b) showed three fitted peaks belonged to C–C(284.8 eV), CO(286.15 eV), and C–N(288.25 eV), while that of WO3 only presented two fitted peaks belonged to C–C(284.80 eV) and CO(286.44 eV) due to the absence of C–N from N-doped graphene material. The XPS spectrum of N 1s of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (Fig. 6c) was fitted peaks at 399.87 eV, 401.60 eV and 402.67 eV, namely pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively [34,35]. O1s of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (Fig. 6d) was split into three peaks at 530.61 eV, 531.31 eV and 532.81 eV, which were related to lattice oxygen, vacancy oxygen, and adsorbed oxygen, respectively. W4f of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO (Fig. 6e) can be fitted to four peaks which are assigned to W6+ (35.90 eV and 37.99 eV), and W5+ (35.46 eV and 37.64 eV), respectively. The presence of the reduced W5+ may be due to the formation of some surface defects of WO3 during the hydrothermal reaction or the appearance of partial oxygen vacancies in the N-GQDs in the complex with WO3. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO showed a shift toward higher binding energy compared to WO3 and WO3/N-GQDs, which may be due to the strong interaction between the double carbon-modified doped graphene and WO3. The excellent electron mobility of graphene-based material facilitated the migration of photogenerated carriers, and made the electron density of the internal electric field of the composite decrease, resulting in the binding energy shift toward high binding energy for WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO.

The relative contents of different oxygen species were roughly estimated by comparing the peak areas of different oxygen species and the binding energy positions of O1s, as shown in Table 1 . The percentages of lattice oxygen, vacancy oxygen and adsorbed oxygen in WO3 were 85.47 %, 13.67 % and 0.86 %, respectively, while those in WO3/N-GQDs were 60.22 %, 30.26 % and 9.52 %. Moreover, the percentage of vacancy oxygen in WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO reached the maximum of 33.36 %, which indicated that high oxygen vacancies can be obtained in the composites by calcination under N2 atmosphere. Table 2 showed the comparison of the W4f binding energy positions and the split peak area of W in different oxidation states. The W4f double peak in XPS spectra belonged to W5+ and W6+, respectively, and we can roughly estimate the relative contents of W5+ and W6+ by comparing the W peak areas of different oxidation states. The content of W5+ in WO3 was 16.90 %, while that in the calcined composites WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO were 23.59 % and 23.71 %, respectively. The presence of W5+ implied the presence of oxygen vacancies in all samples, and the increase of W5+ in WO3/N-GQDs and WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was ascribed to the introduction of N-doped graphene material which provided more doping atoms and defect sites to form higher oxygen vacancies during the annealing process.

Using electrochemical work under UV light irradiation at 365 nm, the instantaneous photocurrent responses of various materials were examined. The effects of various mass percentages of N-GQDs on the composites were compared in Fig. 7 a. A trend was observed that the photocurrent response of the composites exhibited an increasing and then decreasing with the increase of N-GQDs content. When the N-GQDs content was 0.5 %, the photocurrent response reached the peak (this ratio was used in subsequent tests). N-GQDs can operate as a photosensitizer to speed up the separation of electron-hole pairs so that there were more photoelectrons as the amount of N-GQDs increased. When the content was too high, N-GQDs connected to the surface of WO3 would diminish its absorption of photons, resulting in a fall in the photocurrent value. The photocurrent response of WO3/N-GQDs under 365 nm light irradiation was 1.87 × 10−4A/cm2, which was 1.6 times more than that of WO3 (5.5 × 10−5A/cm2). This was because the calcined WO3/N-GQDs contained a high concentration of oxygen vacancies which may trap electrons to prevent them from recombining with holes, and stimulate photoelectrons to the conduction band to increase their lifespan [11].

Compared with WO3 and WO3/N-GQDs, the photocurrent response of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was markedly improved at the initial stage of irradiation, following by a continuous decrease of photocurrent response due to the presence of the recombination process [36]. It was observed that the photocurrent response of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO presented a slow increase from 2.90 × 10−4A/cm2 to 2.96 × 10−4A/cm2 during the irradiation process of three cycles, which was ascribed to the storage of photogenerated electrons by the oxygen vacancies. Only a part of photogenerated electrons were transported to the contact electrode until the deep traps were ﬁlled, resulting in a slow increase of the photocurrent response. Meanwhile, the enhanced light absorption by the cooperative effect of N-GQDs and N-rGO promoted the separation of electron-hole pairs, and the migration of photogenerated electrons to the external circuit was facilitated by the excellent electrical conductivity of graphene, which improves the photocurrent response of the WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites.

To study the electrochemical conductivity of the interface, the electrochemical impedance was tested as shown in Fig. 8 . In the high-frequency region of the impedance diagram, the smaller the radius of the electrochemical impedance spectrum, the higher the conductivity of the material. WO3/N-GQDs exhibited a lower resistance compared with WO3 since the formation of oxygen vacancies could cause trap states [37], capture the photogenerated electrons, and form built-in electric field, while the electrochemical impedance of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was further reduced due to the presence of N-GQDs and N-rGO that could accelerate the electron migration. Therefore, the cooperative effect of oxygen vacancies and double carbon modifications could promote the separation of photogenerated carriers and improve the photoelectric properties of the materials. The results were in agreement with the photocurrent result.

The LSV was evaluated under 365 nm illumination to further investigate the photovoltaic properties of the composite and the results were shown in Fig. 9
. The photocurrents of these materials indicated that they had superior photo-absorption ability and photoactivity under illumination conditions. All samples showed an increase in photocurrent density with the applied electric field, indicating that the charge separation efficiency was improved when the applied electric field was increased [38,39]. Under the light condition, the photocurrent density of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO was 4.28 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V (Hg/Hg2Cl2), which was 2.9 times as high as that of pure WO3 (1.46 mA/cm2). This was attributed to the synergistic effect of the double carbon modification and oxygen vacancies of N-GQDs and N-rGO, which improved the photoelectron capture and promoted the charge separation while facilitating the electron lifetime and electron mobility.

Mott-Schottky was used to study the band potential and carrier concentration of the material. As shown in Fig. 10, the Mott-Schottky curve of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO had a positive slope with the characteristic of n-type semiconductors [40], where the carriers were mainly electrons. A negative slope of the composite appeared at the end of the curve due to the combination of graphene-based materials which exhibited the characteristic of p-type semiconductors. Meanwhile, WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO had a lowest slope, indicating that it had a highest carrier concentration. For n-type semiconductors, the carrier concentration can be calculated according to formula (1, 2) [41–43]. The carrier concentration of pure WO3 was 2.62 × 1020 cm−3. When N-GQDs and oxygen vacancy were added, the carrier concentration of WO3/N-GQDs increased to 3.96 × 1020 cm−3. It suggested that oxygen vacancies in WO3 led to an increase in carrier concentration due to the local surface plasmonic resonance generated by the oxygen vacancies, which can improve the capture ability of photogenerated carriers. At the same time, due to the good electron separation and migration ability of N-GQDs, the electrons and holes of the composites can be transferred rapidly through N-GQDs, thus improving carrier transport efficiency. The carrier concentration of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composite reached 5.78 × 1020 cm−3 through the synergistic effect of oxygen vacancy and double-carbon modification.

B , and T stand for spatial capacitance, applied potential, charged potential, carrier density, Boltzmann constant, and absolute temperature. The dielectric constant (50), vacuum dielectric constant (8.854 × 10−14), and electron charge (1.6 × 10−19) were represented by ɛ, ɛ
0 , and e.

A mechanism was proposed to analyze the oxygen vacancies and double carbon modification of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites (Fig. 11 ). Firstly, N-GQDs promoted the light absorption of the composites and improved the utilization of visible light; Secondly, the excellent electron migration ability of N-rGO enabled the rapid migration of photogenerated electrons generated by the composites. Moreover, oxygen vacancies extended the carrier lifetime to a certain extent, thus realizing the optimization performance of WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites. Finally, under the cooperative effect of N-GQDs, N-rGO and oxygen vacancies, the band gap of the composites was reduced, which was favorable for the generation of electron-hole pairs by photoexcitation. Specifically, electrons were excited by the photon energy under the light irradiation, leaped from the valence band to the conduction band (CB) of WO3, and transferred from HOMO orbitals to LUMO orbitals of N-GQDs. Subsequently, the electrons were migrated from the LUMO orbitals of N-GQDs to the CB of WO3 due to the different energy band structures of WO3 and N-GQDs, and then transmitted to the external circuit by the excellent electron migration ability of N-rGO. At the same time, the oxygen vacancies trapped the photoelectrons to prevent the recombination, thus extending the electron lifetime. The combination of WO3 with N-GQDs and N-rGO provided more defect sites and conductive ways to separate, capture and transport, so that more photoelectrons were transferred to enhance the photoelectric performances of the composites.

WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites were successfully prepared by a simple hydrothermal method, and the effects of graphene-based materials and oxygen vacancies on the composites were investigated. The results showed that N-GQDs and WO3 were successfully attached to the surface of N-rGO, and defects were formed on the surface of the calcined material with increased oxygen vacancy content. The photoelectric properties of the WO3/N-GQDs/N-rGO composites were significantly improved compared with pure WO3, with the photocurrent density increasing from 1.46 mA/cm2 to 4.28 mA/cm2 at 1.23 V, and the carrier concentration increasing from 2.62 × 1020 cm−3 to 5.78 × 1020 cm−3. These performance improvements were mainly attributed to the cooperative effect of N-GQDs, N-rGO, and oxygen vacancies, which can promote the fast migration of photogenerated carriers, and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. This provides an effective strategy to exploit the potential of photoelectric properties of semiconductor materials.

Linhui Luo: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yun Lei: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Can Li: Investigation. Beibei Du: Visualization. Yongqin Wang: Visualization. Yifan Deng: Visualization. Zehui Tang: Resources. Jiong Chen: Resources. Li Lin: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Key Laboratory for Research and Development of new lithium-ion battery materials (2023-028) and Joint Fund of Bijie City and Guizhou University of Engineering Science (BiKeLianhe[2023]12th).

A. Tolosana-Moranchel, N. Pichel, H. Lubarsky, J.A. Byrne, P. Fernandez-Ibanez Journal of Environmental Chemical Engineering, 2022, 10, 107955.
J. Ruan, Z. Yang, H. Zhang, J. Qiu, Z. Song, D. Zhou Optics Letters, 2018, 43, 3885-3888.
G. Alva, L. Liu, X. Huang, G. Fang Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, 2017, 68, 693-706.
J. Yang, P. Chen, J. Dai, Y. Chen, L. Rong, D. Wang Energy Conversion and Management, 2021, 247, 114767.
